true_relatives.note: Sanskrit svapna (sleep/dream) shares the same PIE root as सोना; this root also gives English 'somnolent' and 'hypnosis'
Latin somnus is a true relative — both come from PIE *swep- meaning to sleep; gives English 'insomnia'

useful_coincidences.note: सो (so) sounds like the start of 'snooze' — both evoke drifting off to sleep

sound_alikes.note: The root form सो sounds exactly like the English word 'so' — imagine saying 'so... I'll sleep now' as you drift off
Latin somnus (sleep) is a true relative — the 'son-' in सोना connects directly to 'somnus', 'insomnia', and 'somnolent'

root_breakdown.logic: The same root gave Latin somnus (sleep), Greek hupnos (sleep, source of 'hypnosis'), and Sanskrit svap — all meaning sleep. Hindi सोना is a direct descendant.

etymology_story: The PIE root *swep- meaning 'to sleep' is one of the oldest verbs in the language family, shared by Latin, Greek, and Sanskrit.
Sanskrit svap became the Prakrit sova, and eventually the simple Hindi form सोना.
Interestingly, Hindi uses the same word सोना for gold — an unrelated coincidence of sound that makes the word doubly memorable.

pronunciation_guide.stress: even and light; the o is a clean, round vowel

tips.tip: the first syllable सो sounds exactly like the English word 'so'

memory_hook.primary: A person curled up on a golden mattress with gold coins scattered around them, fast asleep
memory_hook.connection: The golden mattress links the two meanings of सोना — to sleep and gold — in one image, making both unforgettable

examples.hindi: मैं रात को जल्दी सोता हूँ।
examples.romanisation: maĩ rāt ko jaldī sotā hū̃.
examples.english: I go to sleep early at night.
examples.literal: I night at early sleep am

examples.hindi: बच्चा अभी सो रहा है।
examples.romanisation: baccā abhī so rahā hai.
examples.english: The child is sleeping right now.
examples.literal: child right-now sleeping is

examples.hindi: नहाने के बाद मुझे बहुत नींद आई।
examples.romanisation: nahāne ke bād mujhe bahut nī̃d āī.
examples.english: After bathing, I felt very sleepy.
examples.literal: bathing of after me-to very sleep came

examples.hindi: सो जाओ, कल उठना जल्दी है।
examples.romanisation: so jāo, kal uṭhnā jaldī hai.
examples.english: Go to sleep, tomorrow we have to get up early.
examples.literal: sleep go, tomorrow waking-up early is

words.english: to wake up / to get up

forms.label: causative — to make someone get up

true_relatives.note: Sanskrit utthāna (rising up) is directly related — the same root gives Hindi उठना and the more formal उत्थान used in literary contexts

useful_coincidences.note: उठना literally means to go up — and 'up' captures the core direction of the action perfectly

sound_alikes.note: The 'uṭh' in उठना sounds like the start of 'utter' — imagine uttering 'I'm up!' as you rise from bed

evolution.meaning: to get up, to rise, to wake up

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit compound 'up + to stand' is the literal meaning of getting up — and Hindi उठना preserves this perfectly: rising from a lying or sitting position.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit root combines ut- (up, upward) with sthā (to stand) — literally 'to stand up'.
In Prakrit this collapsed into the simpler uṭṭha, and eventually Hindi उठना.
In everyday Hindi, उठना covers waking up from sleep, getting up from sitting, and rising in the broader sense.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable; the ṭh is an aspirated retroflex stop

tips.tip: the 'uṭh' part is close to the start of 'utter' — the same short u vowel followed by a t-like sound

memory_hook.primary: An alarm clock on a bedside table pointing an arrow straight upward, with a figure rising from the bed toward the arrow
memory_hook.connection: The upward arrow encodes the Sanskrit meaning 'to stand up / rise', which is the literal root of उठना

examples.hindi: मैं हर रोज़ सात बजे उठता हूँ।
examples.romanisation: maĩ har roz sāt baje uṭhtā hū̃.
examples.english: I wake up at seven every day.
examples.literal: I every day seven o'clock wake-up am

examples.hindi: सो जाओ, कल जल्दी उठना है।
examples.romanisation: so jāo, kal jaldī uṭhnā hai.
examples.english: Go to sleep, we have to get up early tomorrow.
examples.literal: sleep go, tomorrow early wake-up is

examples.hindi: वह देर से उठी और नहाने गई।
examples.romanisation: vah der se uṭhī aur nahāne gaī.
examples.english: She got up late and went to bathe.
examples.literal: she late from got-up and bathing went

examples.hindi: बच्चों को उठाना बहुत मुश्किल है।
examples.romanisation: baccõ ko uṭhānā bahut muśkil hai.
examples.english: Getting children to wake up is very difficult.
examples.literal: children-OBL to wake-up very difficult is

true_relatives.note: Sanskrit snāna is the formal/ritual word for bathing — same root family as नहाना, with the nasal preserved differently

useful_coincidences.note: Hebrew nahār (river) sounds remarkably close to नहाना — bathing is what you do in a river; a useful sonic and semantic bridge

sound_alikes.note: Hebrew nahār (river) sounds very close to नहाना — bathing in the river is exactly what the word evokes

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit snā root meant self-washing and ritual purification — the Prakrit ṇhāṇa simplified the initial cluster, and Hindi nah- preserved the nasal.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit root snā meant to bathe, especially in a ritual or purifying sense — स्नान is still used in religious contexts today.
Prakrit simplified the difficult consonant cluster sn- and the word became ṇhāṇa.
Hindi नहाना is the everyday verb for bathing or showering, carrying none of the ritual weight of its Sanskrit ancestor.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light throughout; the middle syllable hā is the longest

tips.tip: the first two syllables na-hā sound exactly like Hebrew nahār (river) — just stop before the r

memory_hook.primary: A person wading into a wide, slow river with their eyes closed, arms raised, letting the water wash over them completely
memory_hook.connection: The river scene connects to Hebrew nahār (river) which sounds like नहाना, locking in both the sound and the meaning of bathing

examples.hindi: मैं रोज़ सुबह नहाता हूँ।
examples.romanisation: maĩ roz subah nahātā hū̃.
examples.english: I bathe every morning.
examples.literal: I daily morning bathe am

examples.hindi: उठने के बाद नहाओ।
examples.romanisation: uṭhne ke bād nahāo.
examples.english: Bathe after getting up.
examples.literal: getting-up of after bathe

examples.hindi: वह अभी नहा रही है।
examples.romanisation: vah abhī nahā rahī hai.
examples.english: She is bathing right now.
examples.literal: she right-now bathing is

examples.hindi: गर्मी में ठंडे पानी से नहाना अच्छा लगता है।
examples.romanisation: garmī mẽ ṭhaṇḍe pānī se nahānā acchā lagtā hai.
examples.english: Bathing with cold water in summer feels good.
examples.literal: heat in cold water with bathing good feels

true_relatives.note: Hindi खिड़की likely derives from a Persian or regional word for a small opening — the kh- initial is characteristic of Persian loanwords in Hindi

useful_coincidences.note: खिड़की sounds like 'khid-key' — a window is something you open with a key, and both crack and window share the idea of a gap that lets light in
The ending -kī in खिड़की sounds like 'key' — a window is something you open, just like a door with a key

sound_alikes.note: खिड़की sounds remarkably like 'quirky' — picture a quirky round window in an unusual shape to lock in the sound and meaning

root.meaning: a small opening, a hole that lets in light

evolution.meaning: window — a small framed opening in a wall

root_breakdown.logic: A window is fundamentally a small hole in a wall that lets in light and air — the Persian root for a small opening became the Hindi word for the framed version of that idea.

etymology_story: The word खिड़की most likely enters Hindi via Persian, from a root meaning a small opening or gap.
The diminutive -kī ending is a Hindi feminine suffix that often indicates a smaller version of something.
Today खिड़की simply means window — the framed opening in a wall that you can open and close.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light throughout; the ṛ is a retroflex flap

tips.tip: खिड़की sounds almost exactly like the English word 'quirky' — same rhythm and similar consonants

memory_hook.primary: A quirky, oddly-shaped round window being pushed open, and bright sunlight flooding into a dark room
memory_hook.connection: 'Quirky' sounds like खिड़की — the strange window shape locks in the sound, while the flooding light encodes the act of opening it

examples.hindi: थोड़ी हवा के लिए खिड़की खोलो।
examples.romanisation: thoṛī havā ke lie khiṛkī kholo.
examples.english: Open the window for some fresh air.
examples.literal: little air of for window open

examples.hindi: उसने सुबह उठकर खिड़की खोली।
examples.romanisation: usne subah uṭhkar khiṛkī kholī.
examples.english: She got up in the morning and opened the window.
examples.literal: she-ERG morning getting-up window opened

examples.hindi: गर्मी है — खिड़कियाँ खोलो और पंखा चलाओ।
examples.romanisation: garmī hai — khiṛkiyā̃ kholo aur pankhā calāo.
examples.english: It's hot — open the windows and turn on the fan.
examples.literal: heat is — windows open and fan run

examples.hindi: क्या मैं खिड़की खोल सकता हूँ?
examples.romanisation: kyā maĩ khiṛkī khol saktā hū̃?
examples.english: May I open the window?
examples.literal: what I window open can am

true_relatives.note: Hindi दरवाज़ा is borrowed directly from Persian darvāza — completely unchanged in form and meaning
Hindi बंद also comes from Persian band — meaning tied shut or closed; this is the same word as in 'bandana' (a bound cloth)
Identical phrase in Urdu — same Persian-origin words

useful_coincidences.note: बंद sounds identical to English 'band' — when the door is banded shut, it's बंद

sound_alikes.note: बंद (closed) sounds exactly like English 'band' — picture a band of tape sealing a door shut
दरवाज़ा begins with 'dar' — Russian дар means 'gift', and in Delhi you'll hear darvāzā constantly; the dar- opening anchors the first syllable

root_breakdown.logic: Persian dar (door) combined with a suffix meaning 'opening' to form darvāza — a word that entered Hindi unchanged and became the universal word for door.

etymology_story: दरवाज़ा comes from Persian dar (door) combined with an element meaning opening or passageway.
Persian had an enormous influence on Hindi during the Mughal period — architectural words like दरवाज़ा entered the language and never left.
बंद also comes from Persian, meaning tied or closed — so the whole phrase दरवाज़ा बंद करना is pure Persian ancestry in Hindi form.

pronunciation_guide.syllables: dar · vā · zā band kar · nā
pronunciation_guide.stress: light throughout; the z in दरवाज़ा is a voiced fricative like English z

tips.tip: the first syllable dar sounds like English 'bar' with a d — dar-vaa-za
बंद is pronounced exactly like English 'band' — band the door shut

memory_hook.primary: A large wooden door with a thick band of tape stretched across it, sealing it completely shut
memory_hook.connection: The band of tape encodes बंद (closed, from Persian 'band' meaning tied/bound), while the door image anchors दरवाज़ा

examples.hindi: बाहर जाते वक़्त दरवाज़ा बंद करो।
examples.romanisation: bāhar jāte vaqt darvāzā band karo.
examples.english: Close the door when going out.
examples.literal: outside going time door closed do

examples.hindi: उसने जाते-जाते दरवाज़ा बंद किया।
examples.romanisation: usne jāte-jāte darvāzā band kiyā.
examples.english: She closed the door on her way out.
examples.literal: she-ERG going-going door closed did

examples.hindi: रात को सोने से पहले सभी दरवाज़े बंद करो।
examples.romanisation: rāt ko sone se pahle sabhī darvāze band karo.
examples.english: Close all the doors before sleeping at night.
examples.literal: night at sleeping from before all doors closed do

examples.hindi: दरवाज़ा बंद है — खिड़की से आओ।
examples.romanisation: darvāzā band hai — khiṛkī se āo.
examples.english: The door is closed — come through the window.
examples.literal: door closed is — window from come
